text: The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the gastropatic ligament up to the level of the right crus. The hernia sac is grasped and gradually reduced into the abdominal cavity using a combination of blunt dissection and harmonic scalpel, maintaining an avascular plane.
The surgeon dissects at the base of the crura to identify the decussation, then proceeds to take down the elongated short gastric vessels up to the level of the left crura.

The surgeon passes a Penrose drain behind the esophagus for retraction, exposing the left and right crura and cleaning the decussation. Medi-central dissection of the esophagus is performed using blunt dissection and harmonic scalpel, extending up to the inferior pulmonary veins to achieve a tension-free repair and mobilize the esophagus intra-abdominally by three to four centimeters.

The esophagus is visible below, with the pericardium positioned at the top of the screen. The dissection progresses to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins, first revealing the left inferior pulmonary vein, followed by the right inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon notes the ample intra-abdominal esophageal length before performing the crural repair using O-ethabon sutures.
The surgeon places lateral sutures to re-approximate the hiatus, ensuring proper alignment and closure of the hiatal opening.

The surgeon passes the fundus of the stomach posterior to the esophagus and performs the classic shoe shine maneuver. The fundus is then tacked to the diaphragm on both sides, setting up the Toupet fundoplication. Two O-ethabon sutures are used for the fundoplication, with a total of four sutures placed from the esophagus to the stomach, two on each side.
